anchor: ildouts increases without matched supply
cite: Electricity markets also rely on a type of single clearing price mechanism called “locational marginal price (LMP)” in which grid operators collect demand requests from utilities and bids from generators. Operators accept bids from cheapest to most expensive until demand is met. The price of that last (most expensive) accepted bid becomes the uniform price paid to every supplier, regardless of what they actually bid. As data centers drive up demand, more expensive generators (at the end of the queue) are needed, driving up the total cost of electricity. See Brian Shearer, How to Lower Electricity Bills to $100 a Month (Vanderbilt Policy Accelerator, 2026), https://law.vanderbilt.edu/new-vpa-report-shows-policymakers-could-bring-down-electricity-bills-with-minor-tweaks/.
